_readme: 'include' / 'exclude' define what counts as user-facing copy. Keep this
tight: linting engineering internals produces noise, and a noisy linter
'allow' entries are EXEMPTIONS. Every entry needs a written 'reason' — the
linter refuses to run if one is missing. An exemption is a decision someone
has to defend later, so it should read like a decision, not like a mute.
Match on any combination of: file (glob), rule (id or '*'), phrase (substring
of the matched text). Narrower is better — prefer file+rule+phrase over a

allow.reason: The legal pages exist to DESCRIBE the constraints — /legal/risk has to be able to say 'we do not guarantee returns' and 'past performance is not indicative of future performance'. Negating a prohibited claim requires naming it. These pages are hand-reviewed, not templated, so the replication risk that motivates the linter does not apply.
The methodology page explains that scores are computed forward-only and that we do NOT publish backtests. Rule 4 prohibits publishing derived performance statistics, not disclosing their absence.

'30-day money-back guarantee' is a factual refund term, not a returns promise. Belt-and-braces: the 'guaranteed' patterns are already gated on returns/profit/gains nouns so this should not fire, but the exemption documents the intent.

Rule 6's single exception: a factual statement about the user's OWN real trial expiry. The banner reports days remaining on a trial the user actually started. NOTE — a separate finding stands against this file's escalating red styling (see PR body); the exemption covers the FACT of the countdown text, not the alarm styling.
Same Rule 6 trial-expiry exception — reports the user's own real trial end date.

_knownViolations_readme: PRE-EXISTING findings, carried so the linter can be switched on TODAY without
a repo-wide copy rewrite (which would collide with work in flight).
These are NOT exemptions. They print on every run and are listed in the PR
that introduced this linter. The ledger is meant to shrink to zero. Adding a
NEW entry here to get a build green is an abuse of the mechanism — fix the
Entries are pinned by file+rule+phrase so that new violations of the same
rule in the same file still fail the build.
PRUNE STALE ENTRIES. When the underlying copy is fixed, DELETE the entry in
the same PR. A ledger entry that no longer matches anything is not harmless:
it silently downgrades a REINTRODUCED violation from blocking to a warning,
so the regression this linter exists to catch would ship green. Entries with
no 'phrase' are the widest — they mute an entire rule for a whole file.

knownViolations.reason: PRIORITY. Trial-summary and recap emails render 'Best pick this trial: <SYM> · alpha vs SPY +N%'. This is a superlative evaluative label on a specific security inside a 1:1 templated email, and it reports how a security performed — Rules 1, 2 and 7. Needs a copy fix by the email owner (suggested: drop the superlative and report activity, e.g. 'N picks logged during your trial'). Not fixed here to avoid colliding with concurrent email work.

Glossary row defining the alpha column ('Positive = beat the market; negative = lagged it'). Definitional rather than promotional, but 'beat the market' is a Rule 1 banned phrase and a neutral rewrite exists ('Positive = ahead of SPY that session; negative = behind'). Owned by the scorecard workstream.

Editorial critique quoting what competitors claim ('Every stock scanner claims to beat the market. Almost none publish a track record you can audit'). The phrase is used to attack the claim, not make it. Low risk; left in place pending an editorial pass.
Names Zacks' own published rating label ('Strong Buy') in a comparison. Naming a competitor's product feature is not Tapeline rating a security.
Names TradingView's technical-rating output ('Buy/Sell/Strong Buy from technicals only') in a feature-comparison row. Describes a competitor's UI, not a Tapeline recommendation.

Reports the academic literature on congressional trading ('studies are mixed; some senate trades have historically beaten the market') and immediately disclaims that Tapeline makes no such claim. Statement about third-party research, not about Tapeline. Flagged for an editorial pass to soften the phrasing.

Hand-written editorial ('a fundamentally strong name that the market hasn't priced in'). A genuine Rule 2 evaluative adjective, but in one-off prose rather than a template, so it does not replicate. Queued for the editorial pass.
Quotes the phrase in order to criticise it ('the "back-tested results" in marketing ... don't actually track it'). Tapeline publishes no backtest; the post argues against relying on them.
